1: YHWH asks: where is your mother's certificate of divorce? Or which of my creditors have I sold you to? The divorce-certificate (sefer keritut) of Deuteronomy 24:1 was a legal document formally ending the marriage covenant. YHWH's rhetorical question denies that any such document exists: Israel's exile was not a formal covenant divorce but a disciplinary consequence of Israel's own sin.
2: Why was no one there when I came? When I called, why did no one answer? Is my hand too short to redeem? YHWH's coming with no one to answer inverts the covenant call-and-response pattern. The dried-up sea and rivers imagery alludes to the Exodus parting of the Red Sea — YHWH dried waters then and can do it again. His power has not diminished.
3: I clothe the heavens with darkness and make sackcloth their covering. Cosmic mourning-dress signals the day-of-the-LORD theophany pattern. Sackcloth is the garment of grief and penitence throughout the ANE; when YHWH clothes the sky in it, creation mourns in solidarity with covenant judgment.
4: The Lord YHWH has given me the tongue of those who are taught (limmudim) so that I may know how to sustain with a word the one who is weary — morning by morning he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught. The Third Servant Song opens (Isaiah 50:4–9). Limmudim (discipled ones) is the same root as Isaiah 8:16 — those formed in the prophetic school. Morning-by-morning awakening is the spiritual discipline of daily divine formation, contrasted with the people who would not listen (Isaiah 50:2).
5: The Lord YHWH has opened my ear and I was not rebellious — I turned not backward. The Servant's open ear directly contrasts with Israel's closed ear of Isaiah 48:8. Non-rebellion and non-retreat are the Servant's fundamental posture — complete submission to the one who sends, even before the mission's cost is revealed.
6: I gave my back to those who strike and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard — I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting. The three actions — flogging, beard-pulling, spitting — are each severe honor-shame violations in the ANE. Beard-pulling was a profound insult to masculine dignity. Spitting in the face was the ultimate ritual humiliation. The Servant accepts all three deliberately, without resistance. The Gospel accounts of Jesus's trial (Mark 14:65; Matthew 26:67; John 18:22) explicitly fulfill this.
7: But the Lord YHWH helps me — therefore I have not been disgraced. Therefore I have set my face like a flint and I know I shall not be put to shame. The flint-face image connotes immovable resolve in the face of humiliation. Luke 9:51 echoes this in describing Jesus setting his face toward Jerusalem. The Servant's confidence is not self-generated but grounded in divine vindication — the honor-shame calculus is inverted.
8: He who vindicates me is near — who will contend with me? Let us stand up together. Who is my adversary? Let him come near to me. Legal language saturates the verse: vindicator (matzdiki), contend (yarib), adversary (ba'al mishpat). The Servant issues a courtroom challenge — assured of his vindicator's presence, he welcomes any accuser to make their case.
9: Behold, the Lord YHWH helps me — who will declare me guilty? They will all wear out like a garment; the moth will eat them. The moth-and-garment decay image appears in Job and Psalms as a figure for the fragility of the wicked. The Servant's accusers, however powerful in the short term, have the substance of fabric — they will disintegrate.
10: Who among you fears YHWH and obeys the voice of his servant? Let him who walks in darkness and has no light trust in the name of YHWH and rely on his God. Two communities are distinguished: those who fear YHWH walk in darkness without any experiential light — yet they trust. This is the pattern of faith without sight, the condition of the exile community that cannot yet see the promised return.
11: Behold, all you who kindle a fire — who equip yourselves with burning torches — walk by the light of your fire and by the torches you have lit. This you shall have from my hand: you shall lie down in maschit (a place of destruction/torment). The self-lit fire represents human-originated religious or political schemes — Babylonian astrology, Egyptian alliances, any alternative to trust in YHWH. Those who navigate by their own light receive their own light's outcome: destruction.
